ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Beef Dry Hor Fun or dry-fried flat rice noodle dishes with beef. Closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002700) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Beef Dry Hor Fun. Code A0FHR (Noodle dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Beef Dry Hor Fun or equivalent dry-fried flat rice noodle dish with beef.

main_ingredients: flat rice noodles (hor fun / kway teow)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Beef Dry Hor Fun is approximately 350 g, comprising a plate of stir-fried flat rice noodles with sliced beef, bean sprouts, and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Hor Fun / Dry-fried Flat Rice Noodles with Beef — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore Foodo composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for 'Beef Dry Hor Fun' was found in either IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD entries for stir-fried flat rice noodles with beef and HPB Foodo data for Beef Hor Fun (wet and dry variants). The dry variant is characterised by higher fat content due to wok-frying without gravy. Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of noodle base (~55%), beef (~25%), and vegetables/sauce (~20%). Energy and macros are consistent with published values for Gon Chao Niu He in regional food composition databases.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to dry-fried preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 40–53 for boiled rice noodles (flat type). A GI of 50 is used as the central estimate for the flat rice noodle base (hor fun). Dry-frying with oil and protein (beef, egg) does not substantially raise GI relative to the noodle base; the mixed-dish GI is estimated at 50 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 61.25) / 100 = 30.6 g (serving carbohydrate: 17.5 g/100g × 350 g = 61.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard plate and request reduced soy sauce, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 1,470 mg sodium — nearly 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from flat rice noodle base despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index of flat rice noodles is low (GI ~50), a full restaurant serving delivers approximately 61 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~31, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to a half-portion and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and wok-frying oil, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A full serving provides approximately 6.7 g of saturated fat and 1,470 mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef cuts, request less oil during wok-frying, and limit frequency of consumption to reduce cumulative saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Dry Hor Fun (干炒牛河, Gon Chao Niu He) is a classic Cantonese-origin stir-fried noodle dish widely popular in Malaysia and Singapore. It consists of broad, silky flat rice noodles (hor fun or kway teow) wok-fried at high heat with thinly sliced marinated beef, bean sprouts, spring onions, egg, and a savoury sauce of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The 'dry' designation distinguishes it from the wet (gravy-based) variant: in the dry preparation, the noodles are tossed directly in the wok without added stock or cornstarch slurry, resulting in a slightly charred, smoky flavour known as 'wok hei' (breath of the wok). The texture is chewy and silky from the noodles, tender from the beef, and crisp from the bean sprouts. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 28.7 g protein, 20.3 g fat, and 61.3 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1,470 mg per serving) due to the soy-based sauces, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the rice noodle base is low (~50), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~31), requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten (oyster sauce contains wheat). It is a culturally significant dish served at tze char (Chinese-style hawker) stalls across Malaysia and Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single exact entry for 'Beef Dry Hor Fun' was found in IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest available regional entries for stir-fried flat rice noodles with beef in both databases. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the flat rice noodle base. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved regional sources and the adaptation methodology is transparent.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo source entries for this dish category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. 4. The is_halal flag is set to false because the dish contains beef, which may or may not be halal-certified depending on the restaurant; the field survey source restaurant (George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer) is a tze char establishment and halal certification is not confirmed. 5. GI is estimated at 50 (low, ≤55) based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled flat rice noodles; the dry-frying method and protein content do not substantially elevate GI. 6. Glycemic load per serving = (50 × 61.25) / 100 = 30.6 g. 7. Cuisine tags from the input (Seafood Dish, Crab, Beer) appear to be restaurant-level tags for the source establishment (George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer) rather than dish-specific tags and have not been carried forward to the food record.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from IMR MyFCD and HPB Foodo composite estimate as no exact match exists.
Applied GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for flat rice noodles (GI 50, low category).
Added mandatory multilingual names: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi for Malaysian/Singaporean cuisine.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes with source justification.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Set gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI 50 ≤ 55 threshold.
Used match_type values only from allowed set: 'exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'.
Excluded restaurant-level cuisine tags (Seafood Dish, Crab, Beer) from food record with explanation in notes.
Set is_halal to false with justification in notes (tze char establishment, no confirmed halal certification).
